content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
March 10, 1977

77-03-01

Scott T. Carey
Cornish and Carey
180 University Avenue
Palo Alto, CA 94301

Re: Opinion Request
    No. 76-087

Dear Mr. Carey:

     You have asked for interim advice pending the issuance
by the Commission of the opinion you requested. Although the
advice provided herein does not necessarily reflect the views of
the Commission, and may in fact be changed by the Commission in
its final opinion, you may rely on this advice and will not be
subject to any enforcement proceedings by the Commission based
on such reliance. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329.

     You have provided me with the following factual infor-
mation by letter and in various telephone conversations. You
are a member of the Palo Alto City Council. As of September 10,
1976, you owned 10 percent of the outstanding stock of Cornish
and Carey, a real estate brokerage firm with several offices in
the Peninsula area. Cornish and Carey is a subchapter S corporation,
which means that the profits of the firm pass through
directly to the shareholders. Prior to January 1, 1977, you
received 18 percent of the profits of Cornish and Carey and on
earnings after January 1, 1977, you are entitled to 24 percent
of the profits. The firm's income is derived from commissions
on real estate sales made by salespersons who are associated
with your firm.

     At my request, you have submitted two form contracts
that reflect the types of agreements Cornish and Carey typically
enters into with its salespersons. These form contracts show
that commissions earned by salespersons are shared between the
salespersons and your firm. Your salespersons apparently do not
receive any salary or expenses from Cornish and Carey, but in-
stead derive all of their income from their share of commissions.
The form contracts also indicate that your firm is obligated to
pay your salespersons their share of commissions "immediately
upon collection or as soon thereafter as practicable" (Form 1-

2.23

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 · EXECUTIVE LEGAL 322-5601 · ENFORCEMENT CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441

Scott T. Carey
March 10, 1977
Page Two


14, para. 7; Form ABS 14, para. 6). Finally, the form contracts
stipulate that it is the intention of the parties to the agreement
that the agreement shall not "constitute a binding either
party" and that the salesperson shall be an independent contractor
(Form I-14, para. 10; Form ABS 14, para. 9). As evidence of
this arrangement, you have advised me that your firm does not
withhold taxes from the commissions paid to your salespersons
and that it makes no contributions to the state workman's compensa-
tion fund on your salespersons' behalf.

In light of all these facts, and because you are required
to file a Statement of Economic Interest by April 1, 1977, you
ask for interim advice respecting your obligation to disclose
the names of the clients of your firm.

Under Government Code Sections 82030(a) and 87207(b)
of the Political Reform Act, if you own a 10 percent or greater
interest in a brokerage firm, you are required to disclose the
name of every person who paid fees to the firm if your pro rata
share of such fee was $1,000 or greater. To calculate the size
of the fee your firm must receive in order to make your pro rata
share $1,000, you may not deduct your firm's overhead. However,
for purposes of completing your Statement due on April 1, 1977,
the staff has concluded that you may deduct the salespersons'
share of fees received by your firm. Based upon our review of
the sample contract you submitted to us, it appears that your
salespersons act as independent contractors and that the firm
never has any legal rights to the salespersons' share of broker-
age commissions.

We also have concluded that the factor you should use
in determining your pro rata share of fees is your ownership
interest in the firm rather than your percentage share of net
profits. Accordingly, you should disclose the names of all
persons who paid your firm commissions between September 10,
1976, when you became a 10 percent shareholder, and December 31,
1976, the closing date of your Statement, of which your firm's
share, not including the salespersons' commissions, is $10,000.
The operative figure is $10,000 because 10 percent of that amount
is $1,000.

For your 1976 Statement, you must disclose the names
of all your firm's clients that meet this threshold, even if
they are located outside Palo Alto. With respect to any sub-
sequent statements you file, however, you are not required to
disclose the name of any client who is located outside Palo Alto,

Scott T. Carey
March 10, 1977
Page Three


not doing business in Palo Alto, not planning to do business in
Palo Alto, or who has not done business in Palo Alto during the
two years prior to the due date of your Statement. This is
because of a recent amendment to the Act that took effect on
January 1, 1977.

If when the Commission issues its opinion in this
matter, it reaches a conclusion different from this staff advice,
you may be required to amend your 1976 statement. Therefore, we
recommend that all necessary records be retained so that you may
make such an amendment if required.

If you need any further guidance in the preparation of
your upcoming Statement, or if you have any questions concerning
this staff advice, do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely yours,

Michael J. Baker
Staff Counsel
Legal Division

MJB:plh

sections.question_synthetic: Must a Palo Alto City Council member who owns 10% of a real estate brokerage firm disclose the names of the firm's clients in their Statement of Economic Interest, and if so, what threshold applies for determining which clients must be disclosed?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Yes, the council member must disclose client names meeting the $10,000 threshold (representing $1,000 pro rata share based on 10% ownership) for the 1976 statement including out-of-area clients, but subsequent statements need only disclose clients doing business or planning to do business in Palo Alto, calculated by deducting independent contractor salesperson commissions from gross fees received.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Interim advice letter (not final Commission opinion); no formally labeled QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections but clear question and answer structure; factual background extensively detailed; recipient advised that staff conclusion may differ from final Commission opinion

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Must a Palo Alto City Council member who owns 10% of a real estate brokerage firm disclose the names of the firm's clients in their Statement of Economic Interest, and if so, what threshold applies for determining which clients must be disclosed?

CONCLUSION: Yes, the council member must disclose client names meeting the $10,000 threshold (representing $1,000 pro rata share based on 10% ownership) for the 1976 statement including out-of-area clients, but subsequent statements need only disclose clients doing business or planning to do business in Palo Alto, calculated by deducting independent contractor salesperson commissions from gross fees received.

embedding.summary: FPPC interim staff advice to a Palo Alto City Council member regarding disclosure obligations for clients of his real estate brokerage firm, clarifying the $10,000 fee threshold and explaining that independent contractor commissions may be deducted when calculating pro rata shares.
